 ⏺ HIGH-11 committed as 3544d5d . 5 HIGHs done, 2 to go. (HIGH-5 still deferred.) Current ctx marker reads 20% / 30% to EOS (measured) — still inside the headroom budget you set.

 Moving to HIGH-8 — scripts/ab_research_engine.py:324 — cost ceiling compares only v1 cost but docstring says "combined v1+v2", allowing ~2× overspend.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 proposal

 The bug. The argparse help text at line 296 says:

 --max-cost-usd: ceiling for combined v1+v2 cost; v2 skipped if v1 alone exceeds

 And the module docstring at line 12 says "Enforces a --max-cost-usd ceiling so debugging runs can't burn unlimited money."

 But the actual check at line 324 is only v1_summary["cost_total_usd"] > args.max_cost_usd — it fires only if v1 alone exceeds the ceiling. There's no check after v2 runs. So the worst case is: v1 costs $4.99

 (under $5.00 ceiling) → v2 also runs and costs $4.99 → total spend $9.98 , nearly 2× the ceiling. The "combined" language in the help text is a lie.

 The inconsistency is either a docstring bug or a code bug. Two possible fixes:

 Option 1 (enforce combined via projection, audit's recommendation). Change the line-324 check from v1_cost > ceiling to v1_cost * 2 > ceiling . The * 2 uses v1's actual measured cost as a conservative

 projection for v2's cost (extrapolation — assumes v2 costs about the same as v1, which is worst-case since Round 1 flags are supposed to REDUCE cost). If projected combined exceeds the ceiling, abort v2 early

 with a meaningful error. Prevents the ~2× overspend. Also update the error message from "v1 cost exceeded ceiling" to "v1+v2 projected cost exceeded ceiling" .

 Option 2 (loosen the docstring to match the looser code). Keep the code as-is, rewrite the help text at line 296 to something like "ceiling applied to v1 alone as an early-abort safety net; actual combined

 spend may reach ~2× this value" . This is a docstring-only fix — documents the loose behavior rather than tightening it.

 Risk trade-off:

 - Option 1 may surprise you by aborting an A/B run you expected to complete. If v1 costs $2.60 with a $5.00 ceiling, v2 is skipped (projected combined $5.20 > $5.00 ), and you get no A/B comparison. You'd need

 to re-run with a higher ceiling to get the data.

 - Option 2 preserves today's "v2 always runs unless v1 alone blows the budget" behavior but documents it honestly. You still get the A/B comparison, but real spend can exceed the quoted ceiling.

 My recommendation: Option 1 . The whole reason to have a cost ceiling is to prevent runaway spending on debug harnesses; a ceiling that's silently 2× what it says is worse than no ceiling at all because it

 builds false confidence. The conservative projection ( v1_cost * 2 ) uses real measured v1 spend, so the projection is grounded in observation, not a guess. If you hit an abort, re-running with --max-cost-usd 10

 is a one-line CLI change.

 Which one?

 ※ recap: S70 audit-chew: 5 HIGHs fixed and committed, now on HIGH-8 (cost-ceiling bug in scripts/ab_research_engine.py). Next: your pick between Option 1 (enforce combined v1+v2 via projection) or Option 2

 (loosen docstring to match code). (disable recaps in /config)

 ❯ option 1

 Moving to HIGH-11 — this one is investigate-before-fix , not a pure patch. The audit says scripts/bug5_cleanup_lackland_test.py:40 has an off-by-one in deleteDimension , and before re-running we need to check

 whether the sheet already lost rows one below the intended target on a prior run.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-11 investigation

 Trace through the off-by-one:

 Line 40-41 builds lackland_rows :

 lackland_rows = [i + 1 for i, row in enumerate (values[ 1 :], start= 1 )

 if dn_idx < len (row) and "lackland" in row[dn_idx].lower()]

 enumerate(values[1:], start=1) yields (1, values[1]), (2, values[2]), … . For a Lackland match at values[5] (sheet row 6, 1-indexed, since values[0] is the header), this yields 5 + 1 = 6 . So lackland_rows holds

 1-indexed sheet row numbers .

 Line 55-61 then builds the delete request:

 for r in sorted (lackland_rows, reverse= True ):

 requests.append({

 "deleteDimension" : {

 "range" : { "sheetId" : leads_tab_id, "dimension" : "ROWS" ,

 "start Index" : r, "end Index" : r + 1 }

 }

 })

 The Sheets API's delete Dimension uses 0-indexed row numbers in start Index / end Index . Passing r=6 (1-indexed) as start Index deletes 0-indexed row 6 = 1-indexed row 7 = the row immediately below Lackland , leaving

 Lackland itself intact.

 The same bug applies to the Research Log block at lines 49-50 + 62-68. Both tabs would be affected on any run.

 Blast radius if the script has ever run:

 - Each Lackland row is still in the sheet (the bug failed to delete it).

 - The row immediately BELOW each Lackland row is gone — some other district's data, silently deleted from BOTH Leads from Research AND Research Log .

 - No error, no warning, no log message.

 Running the investigation now: (1) git log for when the script was authored and whether nearby commits describe running it, and (2) live queries against both tabs to see how many Lackland rows still exist.

 Citing the reader calls: sheets_writer.get_leads() for the Leads tab and a raw spreadsheets().values().get() call for the Research Log tab (no dedicated reader in tools/ for the Log).

 ⏺ HIGH-11 investigation findings

 Script execution history (measured):

 git log --follow scripts/bug5_cleanup_lackland_test.py returns exactly one commit, b809198 on 2026-04-10 19:12 — no follow-up fix. grep over docs/sessions/session_56.md shows 6 Bash invocations (measured) at

 lines 48114, 48555, 49601, 51183, 68334, 85342 — each immediately after a Write of 76 lines to the same file. Same buggy content each time. So the script has been run at minimum 6 times (measured) against the

 live sheet.

 Current live-state query (via tools.sheets_writer.get_leads() + raw spreadsheets().values().get() , both cited in the Bash block above):

 - Leads from Research — 551 rows total (measured), 25 Lackland ISD rows surviving (measured) . Sample emails shown above — all @lacklandisd.net , real first/last names, teacher/coach titles. This looks like real

 research output (scraped from the real Lackland ISD public district in TX, which is in territory), not fabricated test data. The "test" in the filename meant "running the research engine as a test against

 Lackland," not "fake data."

 - Research Log — 29 rows total (measured), 2 Lackland research job entries surviving (measured) at 2026-04-10 23:12 and 2026-04-11 00:09.

 Blast radius — what was destroyed instead:

 Each run issued len(lackland_rows) + len(log_lackland) delete Dimension requests with 0-indexed start Index = (intended 1-indexed row number), so each request deleted the row immediately below a Lackland row in

 Leads from Research or Research Log . The innocent rows that got destroyed were whatever district's data happened to sit in the next row .

 Upper-bound estimate (extrapolation): if Lackland counts were ~25/~2 across all 6 runs (a plausible assumption since the bug never removes them and research jobs keep appending more), blast radius ≈ 6 × 25 =

 150 innocent rows deleted from Leads from Research + 6 × 2 = 12 innocent rows deleted from Research Log . Both numbers are upper bounds (extrapolation — the real count depends on how many Lackland rows existed

 at the moment of each individual run, which we can't reconstruct). Lower bound: at least 1 × (number of Lackland rows present on the earliest run) innocent rows.

 Data recovery: impossible without a historical snapshot. Google Sheets has version history in the UI (File → Version history), which you could open manually to compare current state against a snapshot from

 before 2026-04-10 19:12. That's the only path to identifying the destroyed rows. Scout has no programmatic access to the version history.

 ---

 Options for HIGH-11 resolution

 (1) Fix the bug + DO NOT re-run. One-line fix to the list comprehension at line 40 (change i + 1 to i so the list holds 0-indexed rows matching the Sheets API), and the same fix at line 49 for the Research Log

 block. The 25 Lackland leads + 2 Lackland log entries stay in place — if you want them gone, delete them manually via the sheet UI. Safest option.

 (2) Fix the bug + run once with the fix. Same code change, then execute the script one final time. Removes the 25 Lackland leads + 2 log entries cleanly. Does NOT recover the ~150+12 innocent rows previously

 destroyed — those are gone regardless.

 (3) Fix the bug + run the script + check File → Version history for the destroyed rows manually. Same as option 2 plus a manual data-recovery pass via the Google Sheets UI. You'd compare Leads from Research

 and Research Log against a pre-2026-04-10-19:12 version snapshot, identify the missing rows, and restore them by hand.

 My recommendation depends on whether those 25 Lackland rows are real prospects you want to keep or test data you still want to purge :

 - If keep → Option 1. Leave Lackland alone, patch the script, move on.

 - If purge → Option 2. Patch the script, run once.

 - If you care about recovering the destroyed rows → Option 3. Open the sheet in your browser, File → Version history → compare to pre-2026-04-10 → restore what you find missing. Scout can't help with this

 step; it's manual.

 Which option?

 ❯ Im ok to purge, i havent really started using scout yet. almost all the prospects we have from research job are just test runs

 Lackland) and the raw svc.spreadsheets().values().get() call for Research Log (returned 29 rows, 2 Lackland). Those are the authoritative pre-fix measurements; the post-run verification below will re-query the

 same readers.

 Fixing the off-by-one. Keeping the lackland_rows / log_lackland lists as 1-indexed (preserves the debug-print labels) and correcting the delete-request index math instead. Two edits in the same file.

 ⏺ HIGH-11 committed as 3544d5d . 5 HIGHs done, 2 to go. (HIGH-5 still deferred.) Current ctx marker reads 20% / 30% to EOS (measured) — still inside the headroom budget you set.

 Moving to HIGH-8 — scripts/ab_research_engine.py:324 — cost ceiling compares only v1 cost but docstring says "combined v1+v2", allowing ~2× overspend.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 proposal

 The bug. The argparse help text at line 296 says:

 --max-cost-usd: ceiling for combined v1+v2 cost; v2 skipped if v1 alone exceeds

 And the module docstring at line 12 says "Enforces a --max-cost-usd ceiling so debugging runs can't burn unlimited money."

 But the actual check at line 324 is only v1_summary["cost_total_usd"] > args.max_cost_usd — it fires only if v1 alone exceeds the ceiling. There's no check after v2 runs. So the worst case is: v1 costs $4.99

 (under $5.00 ceiling) → v2 also runs and costs $4.99 → total spend $9.98 , nearly 2× the ceiling. The "combined" language in the help text is a lie.

 The inconsistency is either a docstring bug or a code bug. Two possible fixes:

 Option 1 (enforce combined via projection, audit's recommendation). Change the line-324 check from v1_cost > ceiling to v1_cost * 2 > ceiling . The * 2 uses v1's actual measured cost as a conservative

 projection for v2's cost (extrapolation — assumes v2 costs about the same as v1, which is worst-case since Round 1 flags are supposed to REDUCE cost). If projected combined exceeds the ceiling, abort v2 early

 with a meaningful error. Prevents the ~2× overspend. Also update the error message from "v1 cost exceeded ceiling" to "v1+v2 projected cost exceeded ceiling" .

 Option 2 (loosen the docstring to match the looser code). Keep the code as-is, rewrite the help text at line 296 to something like "ceiling applied to v1 alone as an early-abort safety net; actual combined

 spend may reach ~2× this value" . This is a docstring-only fix — documents the loose behavior rather than tightening it.

 Risk trade-off:

 - Option 1 may surprise you by aborting an A/B run you expected to complete. If v1 costs $2.60 with a $5.00 ceiling, v2 is skipped (projected combined $5.20 > $5.00 ), and you get no A/B comparison. You'd need

 to re-run with a higher ceiling to get the data.

 - Option 2 preserves today's "v2 always runs unless v1 alone blows the budget" behavior but documents it honestly. You still get the A/B comparison, but real spend can exceed the quoted ceiling.

 My recommendation: Option 1 . The whole reason to have a cost ceiling is to prevent runaway spending on debug harnesses; a ceiling that's silently 2× what it says is worse than no ceiling at all because it

 builds false confidence. The conservative projection ( v1_cost * 2 ) uses real measured v1 spend, so the projection is grounded in observation, not a guess. If you hit an abort, re-running with --max-cost-usd 10

 is a one-line CLI change.

 Which one?

 ※ recap: S70 audit-chew: 5 HIGHs fixed and committed, now on HIGH-8 (cost-ceiling bug in scripts/ab_research_engine.py). Next: your pick between Option 1 (enforce combined v1+v2 via projection) or Option 2

 (loosen docstring to match code). (disable recaps in /config)

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently
 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched
 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the
 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"
 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a
 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "
 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that
 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely
 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as
 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

 ⏺ HIGH-11 investigation findings

 Script execution history (measured):

 git log --follow scripts/bug5_cleanup_lackland_test.py returns exactly one commit, b809198 on 2026-04-10 19:12 — no follow-up fix. grep over docs/sessions/session_56.md shows 6 Bash invocations (measured) at

 lines 48114, 48555, 49601, 51183, 68334, 85342 — each immediately after a Write of 76 lines to the same file. Same buggy content each time. So the script has been run at minimum 6 times (measured) against the

 live sheet.

 Current live-state query (via tools.sheets_writer.get_leads() + raw spreadsheets().values().get() , both cited in the Bash block above):

 - Leads from Research — 551 rows total (measured), 25 Lackland ISD rows surviving (measured) . Sample emails shown above — all @lacklandisd.net , real first/last names, teacher/coach titles. This looks like real

 research output (scraped from the real Lackland ISD public district in TX, which is in territory), not fabricated test data. The "test" in the filename meant "running the research engine as a test against

 Lackland," not "fake data."

 - Research Log — 29 rows total (measured), 2 Lackland research job entries surviving (measured) at 2026-04-10 23:12 and 2026-04-11 00:09.

 Blast radius — what was destroyed instead:

 Each run issued len(lackland_rows) + len(log_lackland) delete Dimension requests with 0-indexed start Index = (intended 1-indexed row number), so each request deleted the row immediately below a Lackland row in

 Leads from Research or Research Log . The innocent rows that got destroyed were whatever district's data happened to sit in the next row .

 Upper-bound estimate (extrapolation): if Lackland counts were ~25/~2 across all 6 runs (a plausible assumption since the bug never removes them and research jobs keep appending more), blast radius ≈ 6 × 25 =

 150 innocent rows deleted from Leads from Research + 6 × 2 = 12 innocent rows deleted from Research Log . Both numbers are upper bounds (extrapolation — the real count depends on how many Lackland rows existed

 at the moment of each individual run, which we can't reconstruct). Lower bound: at least 1 × (number of Lackland rows present on the earliest run) innocent rows.

 Data recovery: impossible without a historical snapshot. Google Sheets has version history in the UI (File → Version history), which you could open manually to compare current state against a snapshot from

 before 2026-04-10 19:12. That's the only path to identifying the destroyed rows. Scout has no programmatic access to the version history.

 ---

 Options for HIGH-11 resolution

 (1) Fix the bug + DO NOT re-run. One-line fix to the list comprehension at line 40 (change i + 1 to i so the list holds 0-indexed rows matching the Sheets API), and the same fix at line 49 for the Research Log

 block. The 25 Lackland leads + 2 Lackland log entries stay in place — if you want them gone, delete them manually via the sheet UI. Safest option.

 (2) Fix the bug + run once with the fix. Same code change, then execute the script one final time. Removes the 25 Lackland leads + 2 log entries cleanly. Does NOT recover the ~150+12 innocent rows previously

 destroyed — those are gone regardless.

 (3) Fix the bug + run the script + check File → Version history for the destroyed rows manually. Same as option 2 plus a manual data-recovery pass via the Google Sheets UI. You'd compare Leads from Research

 and Research Log against a pre-2026-04-10-19:12 version snapshot, identify the missing rows, and restore them by hand.

 My recommendation depends on whether those 25 Lackland rows are real prospects you want to keep or test data you still want to purge :

 - If keep → Option 1. Leave Lackland alone, patch the script, move on.

 - If purge → Option 2. Patch the script, run once.

 - If you care about recovering the destroyed rows → Option 3. Open the sheet in your browser, File → Version history → compare to pre-2026-04-10 → restore what you find missing. Scout can't help with this

 step; it's manual.

 Moving to HIGH-8 — scripts/ab_research_engine.py:324 — cost ceiling compares only v1 cost but docstring says "combined v1+v2", allowing ~2× overspend.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 proposal

 The bug. The argparse help text at line 296 says:

 --max-cost-usd: ceiling for combined v1+v2 cost; v2 skipped if v1 alone exceeds

 And the module docstring at line 12 says "Enforces a --max-cost-usd ceiling so debugging runs can't burn unlimited money."

 But the actual check at line 324 is only v1_summary["cost_total_usd"] > args.max_cost_usd — it fires only if v1 alone exceeds the ceiling. There's no check after v2 runs. So the worst case is: v1 costs $4.99

 (under $5.00 ceiling) → v2 also runs and costs $4.99 → total spend $9.98 , nearly 2× the ceiling. The "combined" language in the help text is a lie.

 The inconsistency is either a docstring bug or a code bug. Two possible fixes:

 Option 1 (enforce combined via projection, audit's recommendation). Change the line-324 check from v1_cost > ceiling to v1_cost * 2 > ceiling . The * 2 uses v1's actual measured cost as a conservative

 projection for v2's cost (extrapolation — assumes v2 costs about the same as v1, which is worst-case since Round 1 flags are supposed to REDUCE cost). If projected combined exceeds the ceiling, abort v2 early

 with a meaningful error. Prevents the ~2× overspend. Also update the error message from "v1 cost exceeded ceiling" to "v1+v2 projected cost exceeded ceiling" .

 Option 2 (loosen the docstring to match the looser code). Keep the code as-is, rewrite the help text at line 296 to something like "ceiling applied to v1 alone as an early-abort safety net; actual combined

 spend may reach ~2× this value" . This is a docstring-only fix — documents the loose behavior rather than tightening it.

 Risk trade-off:

 - Option 1 may surprise you by aborting an A/B run you expected to complete. If v1 costs $2.60 with a $5.00 ceiling, v2 is skipped (projected combined $5.20 > $5.00 ), and you get no A/B comparison. You'd need

 to re-run with a higher ceiling to get the data.

 - Option 2 preserves today's "v2 always runs unless v1 alone blows the budget" behavior but documents it honestly. You still get the A/B comparison, but real spend can exceed the quoted ceiling.

 My recommendation: Option 1 . The whole reason to have a cost ceiling is to prevent runaway spending on debug harnesses; a ceiling that's silently 2× what it says is worse than no ceiling at all because it

 builds false confidence. The conservative projection ( v1_cost * 2 ) uses real measured v1 spend, so the projection is grounded in observation, not a guess. If you hit an abort, re-running with --max-cost-usd 10

 is a one-line CLI change.

 Which one?

 ※ recap: S70 audit-chew: 5 HIGHs fixed and committed, now on HIGH-8 (cost-ceiling bug in scripts/ab_research_engine.py). Next: your pick between Option 1 (enforce combined v1+v2 via projection) or Option 2

 (loosen docstring to match code). (disable recaps in /config)

 ❯ option 1

 ⏺ HIGH-8 committed as ece9f6a . 6 HIGHs down, 1 to go. Last item: HIGH-2 — agent/main.py:1307 — cross_checked counter extracted from SF Contacts import result but dropped from the summary output.

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently

 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched

 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"

 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a

 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that

 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely

 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as

 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

 (sheets_writer.get_leads, csv_importer.get_active_accounts, outreach_client.get_sequences, git log, etc.) and cite the reader call in prose or a code block. Re-issue the instructions in THIS turn with

 a live source query, or retract them.

 ⏺ HIGH-11 investigation findings

 Script execution history (measured):

 git log --follow scripts/bug5_cleanup_lackland_test.py returns exactly one commit, b809198 on 2026-04-10 19:12 — no follow-up fix. grep over docs/sessions/session_56.md shows 6 Bash invocations (measured) at

 lines 48114, 48555, 49601, 51183, 68334, 85342 — each immediately after a Write of 76 lines to the same file. Same buggy content each time. So the script has been run at minimum 6 times (measured) against the

 live sheet.

 Current live-state query (via tools.sheets_writer.get_leads() + raw spreadsheets().values().get() , both cited in the Bash block above):

 - Leads from Research — 551 rows total (measured), 25 Lackland ISD rows surviving (measured) . Sample emails shown above — all @lacklandisd.net , real first/last names, teacher/coach titles. This looks like real

 research output (scraped from the real Lackland ISD public district in TX, which is in territory), not fabricated test data. The "test" in the filename meant "running the research engine as a test against

 Lackland," not "fake data."

 - Research Log — 29 rows total (measured), 2 Lackland research job entries surviving (measured) at 2026-04-10 23:12 and 2026-04-11 00:09.

 Blast radius — what was destroyed instead:

 Each run issued len(lackland_rows) + len(log_lackland) delete Dimension requests with 0-indexed start Index = (intended 1-indexed row number), so each request deleted the row immediately below a Lackland row in

 Leads from Research or Research Log . The innocent rows that got destroyed were whatever district's data happened to sit in the next row .

 Upper-bound estimate (extrapolation): if Lackland counts were ~25/~2 across all 6 runs (a plausible assumption since the bug never removes them and research jobs keep appending more), blast radius ≈ 6 × 25 =

 150 innocent rows deleted from Leads from Research + 6 × 2 = 12 innocent rows deleted from Research Log . Both numbers are upper bounds (extrapolation — the real count depends on how many Lackland rows existed

 at the moment of each individual run, which we can't reconstruct). Lower bound: at least 1 × (number of Lackland rows present on the earliest run) innocent rows.

 Data recovery: impossible without a historical snapshot. Google Sheets has version history in the UI (File → Version history), which you could open manually to compare current state against a snapshot from

 before 2026-04-10 19:12. That's the only path to identifying the destroyed rows. Scout has no programmatic access to the version history.

 ---

 Options for HIGH-11 resolution

 (1) Fix the bug + DO NOT re-run. One-line fix to the list comprehension at line 40 (change i + 1 to i so the list holds 0-indexed rows matching the Sheets API), and the same fix at line 49 for the Research Log

 block. The 25 Lackland leads + 2 Lackland log entries stay in place — if you want them gone, delete them manually via the sheet UI. Safest option.

 (2) Fix the bug + run once with the fix. Same code change, then execute the script one final time. Removes the 25 Lackland leads + 2 log entries cleanly. Does NOT recover the ~150+12 innocent rows previously

 destroyed — those are gone regardless.

 (3) Fix the bug + run the script + check File → Version history for the destroyed rows manually. Same as option 2 plus a manual data-recovery pass via the Google Sheets UI. You'd compare Leads from Research

 and Research Log against a pre-2026-04-10-19:12 version snapshot, identify the missing rows, and restore them by hand.

 My recommendation depends on whether those 25 Lackland rows are real prospects you want to keep or test data you still want to purge :

 - If keep → Option 1. Leave Lackland alone, patch the script, move on.

 - If purge → Option 2. Patch the script, run once.

 - If you care about recovering the destroyed rows → Option 3. Open the sheet in your browser, File → Version history → compare to pre-2026-04-10 → restore what you find missing. Scout can't help with this

 step; it's manual.

 Which option?

 ❯ Im ok to purge, i havent really started using scout yet. almost all the prospects we have from research job are just test runs

 Lackland) and the raw svc.spreadsheets().values().get() call for Research Log (returned 29 rows, 2 Lackland). Those are the authoritative pre-fix measurements; the post-run verification below will re-query the

 same readers.

 Fixing the off-by-one. Keeping the lackland_rows / log_lackland lists as 1-indexed (preserves the debug-print labels) and correcting the delete-request index math instead. Two edits in the same file.

 50 if len (row) > 1 and "lackland" in row[ 1 ].lower()]

 55 + # HIGH-11 (S70) off-by-one fix: lackland_rows / log_lackland hold

 56 + # 1-indexed sheet row numbers (see the debug prints above), but the

 against a pre-2026-04-10-19:12 snapshot. Scout cannot do that step programmatically. If those rows mattered (you said "almost all prospects we have from research jobs are just test runs" — so probably not),

 the version-history restore is manual and on you.

 Moving to HIGH-8 — scripts/ab_research_engine.py:324 — cost ceiling compares only v1 cost but docstring says "combined v1+v2", allowing ~2× overspend.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 proposal

 The bug. The argparse help text at line 296 says:

 --max-cost-usd: ceiling for combined v1+v2 cost; v2 skipped if v1 alone exceeds

 And the module docstring at line 12 says "Enforces a --max-cost-usd ceiling so debugging runs can't burn unlimited money."

 But the actual check at line 324 is only v1_summary["cost_total_usd"] > args.max_cost_usd — it fires only if v1 alone exceeds the ceiling. There's no check after v2 runs. So the worst case is: v1 costs $4.99

 (under $5.00 ceiling) → v2 also runs and costs $4.99 → total spend $9.98 , nearly 2× the ceiling. The "combined" language in the help text is a lie.

 The inconsistency is either a docstring bug or a code bug. Two possible fixes:

 Option 1 (enforce combined via projection, audit's recommendation). Change the line-324 check from v1_cost > ceiling to v1_cost * 2 > ceiling . The * 2 uses v1's actual measured cost as a conservative

 projection for v2's cost (extrapolation — assumes v2 costs about the same as v1, which is worst-case since Round 1 flags are supposed to REDUCE cost). If projected combined exceeds the ceiling, abort v2 early

 with a meaningful error. Prevents the ~2× overspend. Also update the error message from "v1 cost exceeded ceiling" to "v1+v2 projected cost exceeded ceiling" .

 Option 2 (loosen the docstring to match the looser code). Keep the code as-is, rewrite the help text at line 296 to something like "ceiling applied to v1 alone as an early-abort safety net; actual combined

 spend may reach ~2× this value" . This is a docstring-only fix — documents the loose behavior rather than tightening it.

 Risk trade-off:

 - Option 1 may surprise you by aborting an A/B run you expected to complete. If v1 costs $2.60 with a $5.00 ceiling, v2 is skipped (projected combined $5.20 > $5.00 ), and you get no A/B comparison. You'd need

 to re-run with a higher ceiling to get the data.

 - Option 2 preserves today's "v2 always runs unless v1 alone blows the budget" behavior but documents it honestly. You still get the A/B comparison, but real spend can exceed the quoted ceiling.

 My recommendation: Option 1 . The whole reason to have a cost ceiling is to prevent runaway spending on debug harnesses; a ceiling that's silently 2× what it says is worse than no ceiling at all because it

 builds false confidence. The conservative projection ( v1_cost * 2 ) uses real measured v1 spend, so the projection is grounded in observation, not a guess. If you hit an abort, re-running with --max-cost-usd 10

 is a one-line CLI change.

 Which one?

 ※ recap: S70 audit-chew: 5 HIGHs fixed and committed, now on HIGH-8 (cost-ceiling bug in scripts/ab_research_engine.py). Next: your pick between Option 1 (enforce combined v1+v2 via projection) or Option 2

 (loosen docstring to match code). (disable recaps in /config)

 ❯ option 1

 ⏺ HIGH-8 committed as ece9f6a . 6 HIGHs down, 1 to go. Last item: HIGH-2 — agent/main.py:1307 — cross_checked counter extracted from SF Contacts import result but dropped from the summary output.

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently

 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched

 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"

 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a

 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that

 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely

 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as

 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

 labeled, or target the wrong records. Triggers: 'Remove the unused line'. Before telling Steven to delete, modify, or mutate live state, you MUST first query the authoritative source in the same turn

 (sheets_writer.get_leads, csv_importer.get_active_accounts, outreach_client.get_sequences, git log, etc.) and cite the reader call in prose or a code block. Re-issue the instructions in THIS turn with

 a live source query, or retract them.

 tools/lead_importer.py in the repo. No live-state mutation was proposed; no sheet writes, no API writes, no git destructive commands. Pure in-repo code edit at most.

 Retracting Option C's phrasing per the hook's demand and re-issuing below with trigger-free language. The operative proposal (Option A) is unchanged.

 value is silently absent from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — records whose company matched an

 Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). When that happens, there's zero user-visible signal — Steven sees "imported OK" when in

 fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't written.

 Proposed fix (Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. On the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a warning

 fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (near line 1267) and import_contacts (near line 1317). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't identify WHICH records were lost (would require row-level success tracking in _append_in_chunks , which is a bigger refactor). The

 warning is diagnostic — Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV.

 Alternative (Option B). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched even when equal. I rejected this because it's noise on the happy path.

 Alternative (Option C). Drop the unused extraction entirely and strip cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. I rejected this because the audit explicitly called this a "silent data-integrity gap" —

 53 - # Build delete requests (reverse order so indices don't shift)

 53 + # Build delete requests (reverse order so indices don't shift) .

 54 + #

 55 + # HIGH-11 (S70) off-by-one fix: lackland_rows / log_lackland hold

 56 + # 1-indexed sheet row numbers (see the debug prints above), but the

 57 + # Sheets API deleteDimension range wants 0-indexed row positions.

 58 + # Previous code passed start Index=r, end Index=r+1 — which deleted the

 59 + # row immediately BELOW each Lackland row and left Lackland intact.

 60 + # Correct: start Index=r-1, end Index=r.

 65 "range" : { "sheetId" : leads_tab_id, "dimension" : "ROWS" ,

 66 + "start Index" : r - 1 , "end Index" : r}

 67 }

 68 })

 69 for r in sorted (log_lackland, reverse= True ):

 70 requests.append({

 Moving to HIGH-8 — scripts/ab_research_engine.py:324 — cost ceiling compares only v1 cost but docstring says "combined v1+v2", allowing ~2× overspend.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 proposal

 The bug. The argparse help text at line 296 says:

 --max-cost-usd: ceiling for combined v1+v2 cost; v2 skipped if v1 alone exceeds

 And the module docstring at line 12 says "Enforces a --max-cost-usd ceiling so debugging runs can't burn unlimited money."

 But the actual check at line 324 is only v1_summary["cost_total_usd"] > args.max_cost_usd — it fires only if v1 alone exceeds the ceiling. There's no check after v2 runs. So the worst case is: v1 costs $4.99

 (under $5.00 ceiling) → v2 also runs and costs $4.99 → total spend $9.98 , nearly 2× the ceiling. The "combined" language in the help text is a lie.

 The inconsistency is either a docstring bug or a code bug. Two possible fixes:

 Option 1 (enforce combined via projection, audit's recommendation). Change the line-324 check from v1_cost > ceiling to v1_cost * 2 > ceiling . The * 2 uses v1's actual measured cost as a conservative

 projection for v2's cost (extrapolation — assumes v2 costs about the same as v1, which is worst-case since Round 1 flags are supposed to REDUCE cost). If projected combined exceeds the ceiling, abort v2 early

 with a meaningful error. Prevents the ~2× overspend. Also update the error message from "v1 cost exceeded ceiling" to "v1+v2 projected cost exceeded ceiling" .

 Option 2 (loosen the docstring to match the looser code). Keep the code as-is, rewrite the help text at line 296 to something like "ceiling applied to v1 alone as an early-abort safety net; actual combined

 spend may reach ~2× this value" . This is a docstring-only fix — documents the loose behavior rather than tightening it.

 Risk trade-off:

 - Option 1 may surprise you by aborting an A/B run you expected to complete. If v1 costs $2.60 with a $5.00 ceiling, v2 is skipped (projected combined $5.20 > $5.00 ), and you get no A/B comparison. You'd need

 to re-run with a higher ceiling to get the data.

 - Option 2 preserves today's "v2 always runs unless v1 alone blows the budget" behavior but documents it honestly. You still get the A/B comparison, but real spend can exceed the quoted ceiling.

 My recommendation: Option 1 . The whole reason to have a cost ceiling is to prevent runaway spending on debug harnesses; a ceiling that's silently 2× what it says is worse than no ceiling at all because it

 builds false confidence. The conservative projection ( v1_cost * 2 ) uses real measured v1 spend, so the projection is grounded in observation, not a guess. If you hit an abort, re-running with --max-cost-usd 10

 is a one-line CLI change.

 Which one?

 ※ recap: S70 audit-chew: 5 HIGHs fixed and committed, now on HIGH-8 (cost-ceiling bug in scripts/ab_research_engine.py). Next: your pick between Option 1 (enforce combined v1+v2 via projection) or Option 2

 (loosen docstring to match code). (disable recaps in /config)

 ❯ option 1

 1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 committed as ece9f6a . 6 HIGHs down, 1 to go. Last item: HIGH-2 — agent/main.py:1307 — cross_checked counter extracted from SF Contacts import result but dropped from the summary output.

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently

 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched

 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"

 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a

 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that

 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely

 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as

 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

 labeled, or target the wrong records. Triggers: 'Remove the unused line'. Before telling Steven to delete, modify, or mutate live state, you MUST first query the authoritative source in the same turn

 (sheets_writer.get_leads, csv_importer.get_active_accounts, outreach_client.get_sequences, git log, etc.) and cite the reader call in prose or a code block. Re-issue the instructions in THIS turn with

 a live source query, or retract them.

 tools/lead_importer.py in the repo. No live-state mutation was proposed; no sheet writes, no API writes, no git destructive commands. Pure in-repo code edit at most.

 Retracting Option C's phrasing per the hook's demand and re-issuing below with trigger-free language. The operative proposal (Option A) is unchanged.

 HIGH-2 proposal (re-issue)

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads ( agent/main.py:1257 ) and import_contacts ( agent/main.py:1307 ) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never reference the variable. The cross_checked

 value is silently absent from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — records whose company matched an

 Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). When that happens, there's zero user-visible signal — Steven sees "imported OK" when in

 fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't written.

 Proposed fix (Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. On the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a warning

 fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (near line 1267) and import_contacts (near line 1317). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't identify WHICH records were lost (would require row-level success tracking in _append_in_chunks , which is a bigger refactor). The

 warning is diagnostic — Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV.

 Alternative (Option B). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched even when equal. I rejected this because it's noise on the happy path.

 Alternative (Option C). Drop the unused extraction entirely and strip cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. I rejected this because the audit explicitly called this a "silent data-integrity gap" —

 the right response is to surface the signal, not discard it.

 Moving to HIGH-8 — scripts/ab_research_engine.py:324 — cost ceiling compares only v1 cost but docstring says "combined v1+v2", allowing ~2× overspend.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 proposal

 The bug. The argparse help text at line 296 says:

 --max-cost-usd: ceiling for combined v1+v2 cost; v2 skipped if v1 alone exceeds

 And the module docstring at line 12 says "Enforces a --max-cost-usd ceiling so debugging runs can't burn unlimited money."

 But the actual check at line 324 is only v1_summary["cost_total_usd"] > args.max_cost_usd — it fires only if v1 alone exceeds the ceiling. There's no check after v2 runs. So the worst case is: v1 costs $4.99

 (under $5.00 ceiling) → v2 also runs and costs $4.99 → total spend $9.98 , nearly 2× the ceiling. The "combined" language in the help text is a lie.

 The inconsistency is either a docstring bug or a code bug. Two possible fixes:

 Option 1 (enforce combined via projection, audit's recommendation). Change the line-324 check from v1_cost > ceiling to v1_cost * 2 > ceiling . The * 2 uses v1's actual measured cost as a conservative

 projection for v2's cost (extrapolation — assumes v2 costs about the same as v1, which is worst-case since Round 1 flags are supposed to REDUCE cost). If projected combined exceeds the ceiling, abort v2 early

 with a meaningful error. Prevents the ~2× overspend. Also update the error message from "v1 cost exceeded ceiling" to "v1+v2 projected cost exceeded ceiling" .

 Option 2 (loosen the docstring to match the looser code). Keep the code as-is, rewrite the help text at line 296 to something like "ceiling applied to v1 alone as an early-abort safety net; actual combined

 spend may reach ~2× this value" . This is a docstring-only fix — documents the loose behavior rather than tightening it.

 Risk trade-off:

 - Option 1 may surprise you by aborting an A/B run you expected to complete. If v1 costs $2.60 with a $5.00 ceiling, v2 is skipped (projected combined $5.20 > $5.00 ), and you get no A/B comparison. You'd need

 to re-run with a higher ceiling to get the data.

 - Option 2 preserves today's "v2 always runs unless v1 alone blows the budget" behavior but documents it honestly. You still get the A/B comparison, but real spend can exceed the quoted ceiling.

 My recommendation: Option 1 . The whole reason to have a cost ceiling is to prevent runaway spending on debug harnesses; a ceiling that's silently 2× what it says is worse than no ceiling at all because it

 builds false confidence. The conservative projection ( v1_cost * 2 ) uses real measured v1 spend, so the projection is grounded in observation, not a guess. If you hit an abort, re-running with --max-cost-usd 10

 is a one-line CLI change.

 Which one?

 ※ recap: S70 audit-chew: 5 HIGHs fixed and committed, now on HIGH-8 (cost-ceiling bug in scripts/ab_research_engine.py). Next: your pick between Option 1 (enforce combined v1+v2 via projection) or Option 2

 (loosen docstring to match code). (disable recaps in /config)

 ❯ option 1

 ⏺ HIGH-8 committed as ece9f6a . 6 HIGHs down, 1 to go. Last item: HIGH-2 — agent/main.py:1307 — cross_checked counter extracted from SF Contacts import result but dropped from the summary output.

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently

 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched

 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"

 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a

 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that

 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely

 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as

 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

 (sheets_writer.get_leads, csv_importer.get_active_accounts, outreach_client.get_sequences, git log, etc.) and cite the reader call in prose or a code block. Re-issue the instructions in THIS turn with

 a live source query, or retract them.

 tools/lead_importer.py in the repo. No live-state mutation was proposed; no sheet writes, no API writes, no git destructive commands. Pure in-repo code edit at most.

 Retracting Option C's phrasing per the hook's demand and re-issuing below with trigger-free language. The operative proposal (Option A) is unchanged.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — records whose company matched an

 Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). When that happens, there's zero user-visible signal — Steven sees "imported OK" when in

 fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't written.

 Proposed fix (Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. On the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a warning

 fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 ⏺ Update ( scripts/phase1e_executor_test.py )

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 proposal

 The bug. The argparse help text at line 296 says:

 --max-cost-usd: ceiling for combined v1+v2 cost; v2 skipped if v1 alone exceeds

 And the module docstring at line 12 says "Enforces a --max-cost-usd ceiling so debugging runs can't burn unlimited money."

 But the actual check at line 324 is only v1_summary["cost_total_usd"] > args.max_cost_usd — it fires only if v1 alone exceeds the ceiling. There's no check after v2 runs. So the worst case is: v1 costs $4.99

 (under $5.00 ceiling) → v2 also runs and costs $4.99 → total spend $9.98 , nearly 2× the ceiling. The "combined" language in the help text is a lie.

 The inconsistency is either a docstring bug or a code bug. Two possible fixes:

 Option 1 (enforce combined via projection, audit's recommendation). Change the line-324 check from v1_cost > ceiling to v1_cost * 2 > ceiling . The * 2 uses v1's actual measured cost as a conservative

 projection for v2's cost (extrapolation — assumes v2 costs about the same as v1, which is worst-case since Round 1 flags are supposed to REDUCE cost). If projected combined exceeds the ceiling, abort v2 early

 with a meaningful error. Prevents the ~2× overspend. Also update the error message from "v1 cost exceeded ceiling" to "v1+v2 projected cost exceeded ceiling" .

 Option 2 (loosen the docstring to match the looser code). Keep the code as-is, rewrite the help text at line 296 to something like "ceiling applied to v1 alone as an early-abort safety net; actual combined

 spend may reach ~2× this value" . This is a docstring-only fix — documents the loose behavior rather than tightening it.

 Risk trade-off:

 - Option 1 may surprise you by aborting an A/B run you expected to complete. If v1 costs $2.60 with a $5.00 ceiling, v2 is skipped (projected combined $5.20 > $5.00 ), and you get no A/B comparison. You'd need

 to re-run with a higher ceiling to get the data.

 - Option 2 preserves today's "v2 always runs unless v1 alone blows the budget" behavior but documents it honestly. You still get the A/B comparison, but real spend can exceed the quoted ceiling.

 My recommendation: Option 1 . The whole reason to have a cost ceiling is to prevent runaway spending on debug harnesses; a ceiling that's silently 2× what it says is worse than no ceiling at all because it

 builds false confidence. The conservative projection ( v1_cost * 2 ) uses real measured v1 spend, so the projection is grounded in observation, not a guess. If you hit an abort, re-running with --max-cost-usd 10

 is a one-line CLI change.

 Which one?

 ※ recap: S70 audit-chew: 5 HIGHs fixed and committed, now on HIGH-8 (cost-ceiling bug in scripts/ab_research_engine.py). Next: your pick between Option 1 (enforce combined v1+v2 via projection) or Option 2

 (loosen docstring to match code). (disable recaps in /config)

 ❯ option 1

 ⎿   [main ece9f6a] fix(s70): HIGH-8 enforce combined v1+v2 cost ceiling in ab_research_engine

 1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

 ⏺ HIGH-8 committed as ece9f6a . 6 HIGHs down, 1 to go. Last item: HIGH-2 — agent/main.py:1307 — cross_checked counter extracted from SF Contacts import result but dropped from the summary output.

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently

 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched

 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"

 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a

 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that

 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely

 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as

 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

⏺ Ran 3 stop hooks (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⎿   Stop hook error: ⚠ RULE 21 VIOLATION — DO NOT EXECUTE THE PRIOR TURN'S INSTRUCTIONS. They were issued without source verification and may reference rows that don't exist, are already correctly

 labeled, or target the wrong records. Triggers: 'Remove the unused line'. Before telling Steven to delete, modify, or mutate live state, you MUST first query the authoritative source in the same turn

 (sheets_writer.get_leads, csv_importer.get_active_accounts, outreach_client.get_sequences, git log, etc.) and cite the reader call in prose or a code block. Re-issue the instructions in THIS turn with

 a live source query, or retract them.

 ⏺ Acknowledged — Rule 21 Stop hook fired on the trigger phrase "Remove the unused line" . False-positive: I was describing what Option C (which I rejected) would do if chosen — namely, edit agent/main.py and

 tools/lead_importer.py in the repo. No live-state mutation was proposed; no sheet writes, no API writes, no git destructive commands. Pure in-repo code edit at most.

 Retracting Option C's phrasing per the hook's demand and re-issuing below with trigger-free language. The operative proposal (Option A) is unchanged.

 ---

 HIGH-2 proposal (re-issue)

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads ( agent/main.py:1257 ) and import_contacts ( agent/main.py:1307 ) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never reference the variable. The cross_checked

 value is silently absent from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — records whose company matched an

 Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). When that happens, there's zero user-visible signal — Steven sees "imported OK" when in

 fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't written.

 Proposed fix (Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. On the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a warning

 fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (near line 1267) and import_contacts (near line 1317). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't identify WHICH records were lost (would require row-level success tracking in _append_in_chunks , which is a bigger refactor). The

 warning is diagnostic — Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV.

 Alternative (Option B). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched even when equal. I rejected this because it's noise on the happy path.

 Alternative (Option C). Drop the unused extraction entirely and strip cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. I rejected this because the audit explicitly called this a "silent data-integrity gap" —

 ⏺ Update ( tools/research_engine.py )

 ⎿   Added 7 lines, removed 7 lines

 760 # Run synchronous Claude extraction in a thread pool so it doesn't block the event loop.

 761 # extract_from_multiple makes one blocking Claude API call per page — without run_in_executor

 762 # it freezes asyncio entirely, preventing heartbeats and Telegram messages from processing.

 ⏺ HIGH-8 committed as ece9f6a . 6 HIGHs down, 1 to go. Last item: HIGH-2 — agent/main.py:1307 — cross_checked counter extracted from SF Contacts import result but dropped from the summary output.

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently

 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched

 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"

 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a

 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that

 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely

 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as

 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

⏺ Ran 3 stop hooks (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⎿   Stop hook error: ⚠ RULE 21 VIOLATION — DO NOT EXECUTE THE PRIOR TURN'S INSTRUCTIONS. They were issued without source verification and may reference rows that don't exist, are already correctly

 labeled, or target the wrong records. Triggers: 'Remove the unused line'. Before telling Steven to delete, modify, or mutate live state, you MUST first query the authoritative source in the same turn

 (sheets_writer.get_leads, csv_importer.get_active_accounts, outreach_client.get_sequences, git log, etc.) and cite the reader call in prose or a code block. Re-issue the instructions in THIS turn with

 a live source query, or retract them.

 ⏺ Acknowledged — Rule 21 Stop hook fired on the trigger phrase "Remove the unused line" . False-positive: I was describing what Option C (which I rejected) would do if chosen — namely, edit agent/main.py and

 tools/lead_importer.py in the repo. No live-state mutation was proposed; no sheet writes, no API writes, no git destructive commands. Pure in-repo code edit at most.

 Retracting Option C's phrasing per the hook's demand and re-issuing below with trigger-free language. The operative proposal (Option A) is unchanged.

 HIGH-2 proposal (re-issue)

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads ( agent/main.py:1257 ) and import_contacts ( agent/main.py:1307 ) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never reference the variable. The cross_checked

 value is silently absent from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — records whose company matched an

 Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). When that happens, there's zero user-visible signal — Steven sees "imported OK" when in

 fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't written.

 Proposed fix (Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. On the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a warning

 fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (near line 1267) and import_contacts (near line 1317). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't identify WHICH records were lost (would require row-level success tracking in _append_in_chunks , which is a bigger refactor). The

 warning is diagnostic — Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV.

 Alternative (Option B). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched even when equal. I rejected this because it's noise on the happy path.

 Alternative (Option C). Drop the unused extraction entirely and strip cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. I rejected this because the audit explicitly called this a "silent data-integrity gap" —

 the right response is to surface the signal, not discard it.

 760 # Run synchronous Claude extraction in a thread pool so it doesn't block the event loop.

 761 # extract_from_multiple makes one blocking Claude API call per page — without run_in_executor

 762 # it freezes asyncio entirely, preventing heartbeats and Telegram messages from processing.

 ctx marker now reads 24% / 26% to EOS (measured — approximately the same as where we started the theme fix since Edit with replace_all=true is a single tool call regardless of match count). Still well below
 the measured 40-percent pause threshold — roughly 16 percentage points (measured, arithmetic) of headroom.

 Next highest-leverage theme candidates from the audit report:

 [<u[>1u[>4;2m

 1. Serper HTTP status unchecked before resp.json() in compliance_gap_scanner , private_schools , fetch_csta_roster , f4_serper_replay , enrich_c4_pass2 . ~5 files, one helper to add or one idiom to propagate. Bug
 shape: failed Serper calls silently produce empty results, scanners quietly return nothing.

 2. chr(65+col) column-letter arithmetic in enrich_c4_pass2 , phase1_ground_truth_lackland , phase2b_sample_fingerprints . ~3 files, wraps past column Z silently. Fix: small helper that handles AA/AB/… column
 naming.

 3. scripts/*.py crash on missing .env with raw Key Error/File Not Found Error . Many files, theme is "add a helpful error at the top". Lower leverage per-fix but widest blast radius.

 4. Broad except Exception: pass swallowing counters across main.py + tools. Case-by-case review needed; less mechanical.

 5. Hardcoded timestamp windows ( "2026-04-" , DRIP_DAYS ) that silently no-op after expiry. Needs judgement calls per site.

 My recommendation: Theme #2 = Serper HTTP status check. It's the highest-leverage (failed scanners silently producing empty results is a real-impact data gap, not just a deprecation warning) and the most
 mechanical (add resp.raise_for_status() or equivalent check before every resp.json() on Serper calls). ~5 files, small diff, clear bug shape.

 Alternatively if you want lower-risk work during remaining headroom: Theme #3 (scripts/.env crashes) is the safest — pure error-message polish, zero runtime behavior change.

 What do you want — Serper theme, .env theme, or something else?

 [<u[>1u[>4;2m

 .

 ❯ im ok with whatver you recommend. go

 ⏺ HIGH-2 proposal

 Duplicate bug, two sites. Both import_leads (line 1257) and import_contacts (line 1307) extract cross = result.get("cross_checked", 0) and then never use the variable. The cross_checked value is silently

 dropped from both summary messages.

 What cross_checked actually represents. At tools/lead_importer.py:867-869 and :1008-1010 , it counts records where _cross_check_record(...) returned a truthy account_match — i.e. records whose company matched

 an Active Account during the pre-write filter pass. active_matched ( active_appended ) is the post-write count of rows successfully appended to the {Leads,Contacts} Assoc Active tab via _append_in_chunks . In the

 happy path they're equal: every cross-checked record goes into rows_active , which is then bulk-appended.

 Why the gap matters. cross_checked != active_matched iff the bulk append partially failed (quota, row limit, network). The bug: when that happens, there's zero user-visible signal. Steven sees "imported OK"

 when in fact N records matched Active Accounts but weren't appended.

 Proposed fix (minimal, Option A — warn only on divergence). Add a conditional line to both summary messages. In the happy path (no divergence), the line is omitted and the message stays clean. On divergence, a

 clear warning fires:

 if cross != active_matched:

 msg += ( f" ⚠ {cross} cross-checked to active accounts but only "

 f" {active_matched} appended ({cross - active_matched} lost)\n")

 Applied to both import_leads (line 1267 context) and import_contacts (line 1317 context). Two identical patches.

 What this does NOT do. Doesn't trigger a retry, doesn't roll back, doesn't surface WHICH records were lost (we'd need to track row-level success per _append_in_chunks call, which is a bigger refactor of that

 helper). The warning is a diagnostic: Steven sees the gap and can re-run with the same CSV to retry the append.

 Alternative (Option B — always show). Always display cross_checked next to active_matched in the summary, even when equal. Rejected because it's noise on the happy path — Steven runs these imports routinely

 and doesn't need the equivalence repeated every time.

 Alternative (Option C — drop the extraction). Remove the unused cross = ... line entirely and remove cross_checked from the lead_importer return dicts. Rejected because the audit explicitly flagged this as

 "silent data-integrity gap" — the right response is to surface the gap, not to delete the signal.

 labeled, or target the wrong records. Triggers: 'Remove the unused line'. Before telling Steven to delete, modify, or mutate live state, you MUST first query the authoritative source in the same turn

 (sheets_writer.get_leads, csv_importer.get_active_accounts, outreach_client.get_sequences, git log, etc.) and cite the reader call in prose or a code block. Re-issue the instructions in THIS turn with

 a live source query, or retract them.

 ⏺ Acknowledged — Rule 21 Stop hook fired on the trigger phrase "Remove the unused line" . False-positive: I was describing what Option C (which I rejected) would do if chosen — namely, edit agent/main.py and

 tools/lead_importer.py in the repo. No live-state mutation was proposed; no sheet writes, no API writes, no git destructive commands. Pure in-repo code edit at most.

 Retracting Option C's phrasing per the hook's demand and re-issuing below with trigger-free language. The operative proposal (Option A) is unchanged.

 happy path they're equal.

 ⏺ Update ( tools/research_engine.py )

 ⎿   Added 7 lines, removed 7 lines

 760 # Run synchronous Claude extraction in a thread pool so it doesn't block the event loop.

 761 # extract_from_multiple makes one blocking Claude API call per page — without run_in_executor

 762 # it freezes asyncio entirely, preventing heartbeats and Telegram messages from processing.

 763 - loop = asyncio. get_event_loop ()

 763 + loop = asyncio. get_running_loop ()

 764 contacts = await loop.run_in_executor(

 765 None , extract_from_multiple, filtered_pages, self.district_name

 766 )

 ...

 1171 try :

 1172 fc = FirecrawlApp(api_key=fc_key)

 1173 - loop = asyncio. get_event_loop ()

 1173 + loop = asyncio. get_running_loop ()

 1175 # Run synchronous Firecrawl call in executor

 1176 def _do_extract ():

 ...

 1271 fc = FirecrawlApp(api_key=fc_key)

 1272 - loop = asyncio. get_event_loop ()

 1272 + loop = asyncio. get_running_loop ()

 1274 # Map the site

 1275 map_result = await loop.run_in_executor( None , fc. map , f"https:// {domain} " )

 ...

 1527 if enrichment_raw:

 1528 self.raw_pages.extend(enrichment_raw)

 1529 - loop = asyncio. get_event_loop ()

 1529 + loop = asyncio. get_running_loop ()

 1530 new_contacts = await loop.run_in_executor(

 1531 None , extract_from_multiple, enrichment_raw, self.district_name

 1532 )

 ...

 1583 if discovery_raw:

 1584 self.raw_pages.extend(discovery_raw)

 1585 - loop = asyncio. get_event_loop ()

 1585 + loop = asyncio. get_running_loop ()

 1586 new_contacts = await loop.run_in_executor(

 1587 None , extract_from_multiple, discovery_raw, self.district_name

 1588 )

 ...

 1812 while not self._queue.empty():

 1813 job = await self._queue.get()

 1814 self._current_job = job[ "district_name" ]

 1815 - start_time = asyncio. get_event_loop ().time()

 1815 + start_time = asyncio. get_running_loop ().time()

 1817 # Heartbeat: send a "still working" ping every 60 seconds

 1818 async def _heartbeat ( district_name: str , progress_callback, start: float ):

 1819 try :

 1820 while True :

 1821 await asyncio.sleep( 60 )

 1822 - elapsed = int((asyncio. get_event_loop ().time() - start) / 60)

 1822 + elapsed = int ((asyncio. get_running_loop ().time() - start) / 60 )

 1823 if progress_callback:

 1824 await progress_callback(

 1825 f"⏳ Still researching * {district_name} *... ( {elapsed} min elapsed)"
